content:
Introduction
Agents have already started transforming multiple sectors such as finance, healthcare, IT, education, and many more. Their ability to perform reasoning and take actions enables organizations to automate tasks, reduce operational time, and improve overall efficiency.
In this article, we will build a simple agentic workflow for document-based question answering using a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline. Although the implementation is simple, the article focuses on explaining the complete process step by step — from architecture design to implementation details.
The goal of this article is not only to create a working agent, but also to help readers understand the fundamental concepts behind agent creation, retrieval systems, and document querying workflows.
Architecture Overview
The main components of a document-querying AI agent are the Large Language Model (LLM) and the Vector Database. Together, these components enable the agent to retrieve relevant information from documents and generate intelligent responses to user queries.
In enterprise applications, the database may contain multiple documents such as project reports, technical documents, HR policies, internal procedures, or organizational knowledge bases. These documents are usually stored in local storage or cloud storage systems.
For simplicity, this implementation uses a small paragraph as the data source instead of multiple files. The intention is to explain the complete RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline and agent workflow in a simple and easy-to-understand manner without introducing additional complexity related to document management. However, the same architecture can be easily extended to support multiple files such as PDFs, Word documents, text files, or enterprise knowledge repositories.
Since LLMs cannot efficiently process large documents directly, the documents are first divided into smaller manageable chunks using a text-splitting technique. Chunking improves retrieval accuracy and ensures that the language model receives only the most relevant contextual information.
Each text chunk is then converted into vector embeddings using an embedding model. Embeddings are numerical vector representations that capture the semantic meaning of the text. These embeddings, along with their corresponding text chunks and metadata, are stored inside ChromaDB, which acts as the vector database.
When a user submits a query through the interactive HTML dashboard, the query is also converted into an embedding vector using the same embedding model. The vector database then compares the query embedding with all stored embeddings and calculates similarity distances between them.
Based on semantic similarity search, the top matching chunks (for example, the top 3 nearest embeddings) are retrieved from the vector database. These retrieved chunks are then passed to the LLM as contextual information.
Finally, the LLM processes both the user query and the retrieved contextual data to generate a meaningful, context-aware, and accurate response.
The overall workflow of the architecture can be summarized as follows:
Documents → Text Splitting → Embeddings → ChromaDB Vector Storage → User Query → Similarity Search → Context Retrieval → LLM Response Generation
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Agent ArchitectureProject Structure
The agent is developed using Visual Studio Code (VS Code), and Git Bash is used as the terminal environment for project setup and execution.
The following commands are used to create the initial project folder structure:
cd ~ mkdir rag-agent && cd rag-agent
mkdir app mkdir static
touch app/__init__.py
\ app/main.py
\ app/ingest.py
\ app/retriever.py
\ app/agent.py
touch static/index.html
touch .env requirements.txt
After executing the above commands, the project structure will look as follows:
Folder structurePurpose of Each Folder and File
Understanding the purpose of each folder and file is important before moving into the implementation. A modular project structure improves readability, debugging, maintainability, and future scalability of the AI agent.
ingest.py
This file is responsible for document ingestion and preprocessing.
Main functionalities:

• Load documents or text data

• Split large documents into smaller chunks

• Generate embeddings using embedding models

• Store embeddings inside ChromaDB
retriever.py
This module handles semantic retrieval operations.
Responsibilities:

• Convert user query into embeddings

• Perform similarity search in ChromaDB

• Retrieve top matching chunks

• Return contextual information to the agent
agent.py
This file contains the core agent workflow and interaction logic with the Large Language Model (LLM).
Responsibilities:

• Receive retrieved contextual data

• Combine user query and retrieved chunks

• Send prompts to the LLM

• Generate context-aware responses
static/
The static folder contains frontend files used to create the user interface.
This folder is responsible for:

• User interaction

• Query submission

• Displaying AI-generated responses
index.html
This file contains the HTML code for the interactive web interface.
Responsibilities:

• Create input fields for user queries

• Display generated responses

• Provide a simple chatbot-like interaction experience\
.env
The .env file stores environment variables and sensitive configuration details.
Typical contents:

• OpenAI API keys

• Database paths

• Model configurations

• Secret tokens
main.py
This is the main entry point of the application.
Responsibilities:

• Starts the application

• Connects frontend and backend

• Handles API routing

• Receives user queries

• Sends responses back to the HTML interface
requirements.txt
This file contains all Python dependencies required to run the project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step By Step Implementation
Stage 1: Installation of Required Packages
The main packages used for interaction between the HTML interface and the AI agent workflow are FastAPI, Uvicorn, and Pydantic.Understanding the role of these packages is important to understand how the interaction flow happens within the application. For a better understanding, refer to the image below.
The remaining packages used in the Requirements use case are as follows:
Langchain: Provides the core framework for building LLM applications, chains, and agent workflows.
Langchain-core: Defines the foundational interfaces, runnables, prompts, messages, and output parsers used throughout LangChain.
Langchain-community: Offers community-maintained integrations such as document loaders, tools, and vector database connectors.
Langchain-openAi: Integrates OpenAI models and embedding services with LangChain applications.
Langchain-chroma: Connects LangChain with ChromaDB for storing and retrieving vector embeddings.
langchain-text-splitters: Splits large documents into smaller chunks for efficient embedding and retrieval.
sentence-Transformers: Generates high-quality sentence embeddings using pre-trained transformer models for semantic similarity search.
chromabd: An open-source vector database used to store embeddings and perform fast semantic similarity searches.
Stage 2: The Document Processor, Embedding
Ingest.py used for document processing.
What It Does
ingest.py runs once before your agent can answer any questions. Think of it like indexing a library — you cannot search for a book you have not catalogued. Its job is to:

• Take your raw document text

• Split it into manageable overlapping chunks

• Convert each chunk into a mathematical vector (embedding)

• Store everything permanently on disk in ChromaDB
The Complete Ingest.py
import os
import ssl
import httpx
import urllib3
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_chroma import Chromaload_dotenv()
# Corporate SSL fix — patches both httpx and requests (used by tiktoken)
ssl._create_default_https_context = ssl._create_unverified_context
os.environ["CURL_CA_BUNDLE"] = ""
os.environ["REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE"] = ""
urllib3.disable_warnings(urllib3.exceptions.InsecureRequestWarning)
original_send = HTTPAdapter.send
def patched_send(self, request, **kwargs):
kwargs['verify'] = False
return original_send(self, request, **kwargs)
HTTPAdapter.send = patched_send
DOCUMENT = """
Autonomous Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) represent one of the most
rapidly evolving domains in modern engineering, combining advances in
control theory, computer vision, and artificial intelligence. At the
core of every autonomous UAV lies a flight control system responsible
for maintaining stability, following trajectories, and responding to
disturbances. Traditionally, these controllers were designed using
classical methods such as PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)
control, which adjusts thrust and attitude based on the error between
desired and actual states. While PID controllers are simple and
reliable, they struggle with highly nonlinear dynamics, uncertain
aerodynamic conditions, and payload variations.
To address these limitations, adaptive control methods were introduced.
Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) and L1 adaptive control allow
the controller to modify its parameters in real time based on observed
system behaviour, ensuring stability even when the UAV model changes
due to wind gusts or structural damage. These methods rely on Lyapunov
stability theory to guarantee that the adaptation law drives the system
error to zero asymptotically. The mathematical foundation involves
designing a reference model that represents ideal behaviour, and an
adaptation law that minimises the difference between the actual system
and reference model outputs.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful alternative
approach to UAV control. Unlike model-based adaptive control, RL agents
learn control policies directly from interaction with the environment
through trial and error. Algorithms such as Proximal Policy Optimisation
(PPO) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) have demonstrated impressive results
in simulated quadrotor environments, learning to hover, navigate
obstacles, and recover from severe disturbances. The key advantage of RL
is that it does not require an explicit mathematical model of the system.
However, RL policies trained in simulation often suffer from the
sim-to-real gap, where the learned policy performs poorly in the real
world due to unmodelled dynamics and sensor noise.
Computer vision plays a critical role in enabling UAV autonomy beyond
GPS-dependent navigation. Visual odometry uses sequences of camera
frames to estimate the UAV position and velocity in GPS-denied
environments such as indoor spaces or urban canyons. Object detection
algorithms, particularly those based on the YOLO (You Only Look Once)
family, enable real-time identification and tracking of targets from
aerial imagery. When combined with a gimbal control system, a UAV can
lock onto and continuously track a moving target even while the UAV
itself is manoeuvring. The integration of detection, tracking, and
control into a single pipeline is known as visual servoing.
Path planning is another critical component of autonomous UAV systems.
Given a map of the environment and a goal position, the path planner
computes a collision-free trajectory that the flight controller then
follows. Common algorithms include A-star for grid-based environments,
RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Trees) for continuous spaces, and more
recently, learning-based planners that use deep neural networks to
predict obstacle-free paths directly from sensor data. The planned path
is passed to a trajectory tracking controller that generates the
required thrust and attitude commands at each time step.
Safety and reliability are paramount concerns in UAV systems,
particularly for application
